title: The establishment of the French settlement of Québec
summary: How France claimed the St. Lawrence valley — and why it matters to the Goodwater branch.

tiers.brief: In 1534–1535 Jacques Cartier sailed up the St. Lawrence, visited Stadacona (today's Québec City), and claimed the valley for France as "Canada." France's early colonization was disrupted by religious wars at home until the late 1500s. For our tree, this French doorway leads to the Mary Ann Goodwater branch.
tiers.standard:
In 1534, France's King Francis I authorized navigator Jacques Cartier, a French-Breton maritime explorer, to lead a voyage to the New World to seek gold and other riches, as well as a new sea route to Asia.

In 1535, Jacques Cartier and his crew sailed up the St. Lawrence River and visited an Iroquois settlement of about 500 persons called "Stadacona" on the bluff where Québec City stands today. Cartier named the river the St. Lawrence. While in the New World he claimed the large St. Lawrence River valley for France, calling the area "Canada", after the Huron-Iroquois word "kanata".

Cartier's later expeditions up the St. Lawrence and the building of Fort Charlesbourg-Royal enabled France to lay claim to the lands that would become the French colony of Québec.

Between 1562 and 1598, France's colonization efforts in North America were disrupted by religious wars at home between French Roman Catholics and the rising French Protestant Huguenots.

In 1578, eighty-six years after Columbus reached the Americas, England began its own efforts to explore and settle the New World. The English monarchy and early Parliament had been distracted by wars in Scotland, Ireland, and France, and by Protestant–Catholic conflict at home — leaving little energy for the New World until later in the 1600s.

For the Winifred Coss Family Tree, this is the doorway to the Goodwater branch: French settlers and farmers whose descendants eventually meet the Powers branch in Iowa in 1853.
tiers.deep:
In 1534, France's King Francis I authorized navigator Jacques Cartier to explore the New World. Cartier's 1535 voyage up the St. Lawrence brought him to Stadacona — an Iroquois settlement on the bluff where Québec City stands — and he claimed the river valley for France, naming the country "Canada" from the Huron-Iroquois word "kanata."

Fort Charlesbourg-Royal and later expeditions let France hold its claim to what became the colony of Québec. Meanwhile, between 1562 and 1598, France's colonization was repeatedly interrupted by wars between Roman Catholics and Huguenot Protestants at home — the same era of religious upheaval that shapes the English chapters of this story.

England's own sustained New World push came later: by 1578 the crown was still absorbed in Scotland, Ireland, France, and domestic Protestant–Catholic conflict. The two colonial stories run in parallel for generations before they meet in one family.

For the Winifred Coss Family Tree, Québec is not background colour — it is the origin of the Mary Ann Goodwater branch. French farmers and settlers along the St. Lawrence are direct ancestors on that line. Their descendants carry the name Goodwater forward until, in 1853, Joseph Warren Coss marries Mary Ann Goodwater and the Goodwater and Powers branches join.

title: Salem Witch Trials
summary: Leading ideas from Scotland, colony context, Martha (Barrett) Sparks imprisoned in Boston, and the Barrett line into the Powers branch.

tiers.brief: In 1691, Martha (Barrett) Sparks of Chelmsford was accused of witchcraft and sent to a Boston prison on 28 October, while her husband Henry Sparks was away fighting in King Philip's War. She was released on 8 December 1692 after fifty-eight weeks — a direct family tie on the Powers branch through the Barrett line.
tiers.standard:
Leading context: In the late 1500s European Christians developed a theory that witches made a pact with the devil and worked in groups. King James VI of Scotland (later James I of England) fueled a witch panic from 1590 and wrote Daemonologie in 1597.

In Massachusetts, Salem was settled in the 1620s–1630s. The Barrett family arrived with the Winthrop Fleet and moved to Chelmsford in 1663. The colony executed its first accused witch, Margaret Jones, in 1648. In October 1691 King William and Queen Mary rechartered the colony as the Province of Massachusetts Bay under Governor Sir William Phips — while Puritans feared losing control.

What witchcraft meant: Before 1692 it was a capital crime but hard to prove; accusation alone could ruin someone because prisoners paid their own jail costs. In spring 1692, while Phips was away, courts allowed spectral evidence and created a Court of Oyer and Terminer that assumed guilt until innocence was proved.

The family event: Martha (Barrett) Sparks of Chelmsford was imprisoned on 28 October 1691. Boston jailer John Arnold also held famous Salem prisoners. Thomas Barrett Jr. petitioned for Martha's release on 1 November 1692; she was freed on 8 December 1692 after fifty-eight weeks.

Lagging family line: Rebecca Barrett married Walter Powers III in 1696; their son Josiah Powers Sr. was born in 1710 on Neshoba Hill — the Edith Powers branch of Winifred's tree.
tiers.deep:
Leading — ideas: In the late 1500s a Christian theory held that witches made deliberate pacts with the devil and did not act alone. In 1590, while James VI was king of Scotland, more than a hundred people were accused of plotting his death by witchcraft — including charges that witches caused a storm that sank one of his ships. James published Daemonologie in 1597. Between 1603 and 1625 Scotland held about twenty witchcraft trials involving nearly 450 suspects, with roughly half executed.

Leading — colony: Puritans founded Salem in 1626–1630. Thomas Barrett and Margaret (Huntington) arrived about 1635, moved to Chelmsford in 1663, and their granddaughter Martha (Barrett) Sparks lived thirty miles west of Salem. In 1648 Massachusetts executed Margaret Jones, a Charlestown midwife — the first of about eighty New England witch accusations before 1693, with fifteen executions.

Leading — 1691 government: On 7 October 1691 William and Mary rechartered Massachusetts as the Province of Massachusetts Bay and sent Sir William Phips as royal governor. The charter tightened English control and alarmed Puritans.

What is "Witchcraft"? Dad's document answers this directly. In the 1600s English American colonies, witchcraft before 1692 meant: a capital crime that was difficult to prove; formal accusation alone could punish someone, because prisoners paid all their own jail expenses whether guilty or innocent; cases went through the regular court system with ordinary rules of evidence; if found innocent, the accused could sue the accuser for slander or defamation to recover costs; and many accusations were common curses not meant literally — like calling someone a bad name.

What changed in 1692: In spring 1692, while Governor Phips was away handling Native issues on the western frontier, the rules changed and convictions became easier. Witchcraft was still a capital crime, but courts now allowed "spectral evidence" — testimony that the accused person's spirit appeared in a dream while their body was elsewhere. A special Court of Oyer and Terminer (French for "to hear and to determine") assumed guilt until innocence was proved. Accused witches were shackled, denied food, and pressured to confess through intimidation, lack of legal counsel, and torture. The accused still paid all court costs; if found guilty they forfeited all property.

Event — Martha Sparks: On 28 October 1691 Martha (Barrett) Sparks, daughter of Thomas Barrett Jr., was sent to a Boston prison on suspicion of witchcraft while her husband Henry Sparks fought in King Philip's War. She was never brought to trial. Jailer John Arnold held her for fifty-eight weeks alongside Sarah Good, Sarah Osbourn, and Rebecca Nurse from Salem.

Event — wider panic: Governor Phips left immediately for the western frontier, leaving a leadership vacuum. Ralph Farnham Jr. died in Andover in January 1692 amid the Mary Tyler trial; Ralph Farnham III and John Farnham were summoned in the Martha Carrier trial — Carrier was hanged 19 August 1692 after smallpox devastated her family.

Resolution: On 1 November 1692 Thomas Barrett Jr. petitioned Phips (Massachusetts Archives Vol. 135 No. 64), citing twelve months and five days in prison with no evidence and no accuser, while he cared for Martha's two children and an ill wife. On 6 December 1692 he guaranteed a bond; Martha was released 8 December 1692. Dad's document points to the Jessie Maud Paff family story for more witch-trial history.

Lagging — Powers branch: Rebecca Barrett (born 1673, Chelmsford) married Walter Powers III on 16 December 1696 in Littleton. Their son Josiah Powers Sr. was born 22 September 1710 in a Garrison House on Neshoba Hill — carrying the line to Edith Powers and eventually Winifred Eloise Coss.
